Price discrimination and tiering — student discounts, enterprise vs. starter plans, peak vs. off-peak fares, Good/Better/Best bundles — let you charge each segment closer to what it will bear, capturing value that a single blended price would forfeit. IndiGo charges wildly different fares for the same seat depending on how far ahead and how flexibly you book; that's dynamic price discrimination turning willingness-to-pay into revenue.

Underpinning all of this is a way of thinking, not just a formula: first-principles reasoning. Instead of reasoning by analogy ('competitors charge ₹X, so we'll charge ₹X too'), you break the problem down to its irreducible truths and rebuild from there. What value does the customer actually get? What does it truly cost us to serve one more? What is the customer's real alternative? Elon Musk's famous example was battery packs: everyone 'knew' they cost $600/kWh, but decomposing them into raw materials (cobalt, nickel, aluminium, carbon) priced at market showed a floor far lower — so the received wisdom was wrong, and a cheaper design was possible. In pricing, first principles means refusing to inherit a number just because it's the convention: rebuild the price from the value created and the true marginal cost, and you'll often find both room to charge more and room to serve segments the old price locked out. First-principles thinking pairs naturally with frameworks like SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats) — but treat SWOT as a prompt for honest inventory, not an answer; its output is only as good as the specificity and truthfulness of what you put in each quadrant.
lessons.example: A Pune-based B2B analytics SaaS initially priced cost-plus: servers + salaries per account came to ₹8,000/month, so they charged ₹12,000 (a 50% markup) as a single flat plan. Two problems: large enterprises would happily have paid ₹60,000 because the tool replaced two analyst roles worth lakhs, while tiny startups couldn't afford ₹12,000 at all and churned. Rebuilding from first principles — what value does each segment get? — they moved to three tiers: Starter ₹4,000 (fits a bootstrapped startup's willingness-to-pay and keeps them alive as future upsell), Growth ₹15,000, and Enterprise ₹55,000 with SSO and a dedicated success manager. Same product core, segmented by value. Revenue per blended account roughly doubled because the enterprise tier captured value the flat ₹12,000 had been giving away, and the cheap Starter tier expanded the top of the funnel instead of losing those users entirely.
lessons.code:
Elasticity + a value-based price sketch

Elasticity example (should we raise price?):
  Current: price ₹500, quantity 1,000 units/mo -> revenue ₹5,00,000
  Raise price 10% -> ₹550. Suppose volume falls to 940 (down 6%).

  % change in quantity / % change in price = -6% / +10% = -0.6
  |elasticity| = 0.6 < 1  -> INELASTIC

  New revenue = 550 × 940 = ₹5,17,000  (up ₹17,000)
  Verdict: raise the price - demand is inelastic, revenue rises.

Value-based price (capture a share of value created):
  Customer's willingness to pay (WTP) = value they receive = ₹2,00,000/yr
  True cost to serve one customer         =    ₹30,000/yr
  Competitor / next-best alternative      =    ₹90,000/yr

  Floor  = cost to serve            = ₹30,000  (never price below, long-run)
  Ceiling= WTP                      = ₹2,00,000 (customer walks above this)
  Anchor = next-best alternative    = ₹90,000  (must beat on value/price)

  A value price of ₹1,10,000 sits below WTP (customer keeps ₹90k surplus),
  above the competitor anchor (justified by extra value), and far above
  cost (healthy margin). Cost-plus would have wrongly priced near ₹45,000.
lessons.tryit: Take a product or service you'd consider selling (a course, a SaaS feature, a consulting hour). Write down three numbers from first principles: your true marginal cost to serve one more customer (the floor), the customer's next-best alternative (the anchor), and the value they receive / willingness-to-pay (the ceiling). Set a value-based price between the anchor and the ceiling, and design two or three tiers so a budget segment and a premium segment each pay closer to their own willingness-to-pay. Note how far your price differs from a naive cost-plus number.

mistakes.m: Defaulting to cost-plus pricing, setting price as cost + fixed markup regardless of the value delivered.
mistakes.fix: Anchor price to customer value (willingness-to-pay) and the next-best alternative, not to your costs. Cost is only the floor. Value-based pricing captures the surplus cost-plus gives away.

mistakes.m: Setting one price for all customers, forfeiting revenue from high-value segments and losing price-sensitive ones.
mistakes.fix: Segment and tier (Good/Better/Best, student vs. enterprise, peak vs. off-peak). Price discrimination captures more of each segment's willingness-to-pay than any single blended price can.

mistakes.m: Assuming a price cut always raises revenue (or a price rise always lowers it) without checking elasticity.
mistakes.fix: Check elasticity first. If demand is inelastic (|elasticity| < 1), raising price increases revenue; if elastic (> 1), cutting price may. Measure the actual quantity response before you move price.

quiz.q: A software tool saves a customer ₹5,00,000/year in labour and costs the vendor ₹40,000/year to serve. The nearest competitor charges ₹1,50,000. What does value-based pricing suggest?
opts: Price at ₹60,000 — cost plus a 50% markup
Price between the competitor's ₹1,50,000 and the customer's ₹5,00,000 value, capturing a share of the value created
Price at exactly ₹40,000 to match cost
Price at ₹5,00,000 to capture all the value
quiz.why: Value-based pricing sets the price by the value delivered and the customer's alternatives, not by cost. Cost (₹40,000) is only the floor; the competitor's ₹1,50,000 is the anchor to beat on value; the customer's ₹5,00,000 value is the ceiling. Pricing between the anchor and ceiling (e.g. ₹2–3 lakh) captures real value while leaving the customer surplus. Cost-plus at ₹60,000 gives the value away; pricing at the full ₹5,00,000 leaves the customer no surplus, so they'd likely walk.
quiz.approach: Anchor price to value and alternatives, not cost. Leave the customer some surplus so the deal is clearly worth it, but capture far more than cost-plus would.

quiz.q: A retailer raises price 10% and unit sales fall 4%. What is the price elasticity of demand, and what does it imply for revenue?
opts: Elasticity −0.4 (inelastic); revenue rises after the price increase
Elasticity −2.5 (elastic); revenue falls after the price increase
Elasticity −4.0 (elastic); revenue is unchanged
Elasticity 0 (perfectly inelastic); revenue is unchanged
quiz.why: Elasticity = %ΔQuantity ÷ %ΔPrice = −4% ÷ +10% = −0.4. Its absolute value (0.4) is below 1, so demand is inelastic — quantity fell proportionally less than price rose. When demand is inelastic, a price increase raises total revenue (here the price gain outweighs the small volume loss). The other options miscompute the ratio.
quiz.approach: Compute elasticity as the ratio of percentage changes. |elasticity| < 1 means a price rise increases revenue; > 1 means it reduces revenue.

quiz.q: What best describes reasoning from first principles when setting a price, versus reasoning by analogy?
opts: Copying whatever the market leader charges because it must be correct
Breaking the decision down to fundamentals — true cost to serve, the customer's real alternative, and the value delivered — and rebuilding the price from those
Always charging the lowest price in the market to win share
Adding a standard industry markup to your costs
quiz.why: First-principles reasoning decomposes a problem into its irreducible truths and rebuilds from them, rather than inheriting a number by analogy ('competitors charge X, so we will too'). For pricing that means grounding the price in true marginal cost, the customer's genuine next-best alternative, and the value actually delivered. Copying the leader and applying a standard markup are both reasoning by analogy; always pricing lowest ignores value entirely.
quiz.approach: When a number is 'just how it's done', decompose it. Rebuild price from value, real alternatives, and true cost — you often find room to charge more and to serve new segments.
